Maladaptive Strategies.value: I try to erase the memory of the event
try hard not think about the trauma

Threat.value: Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again
Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively

Memory.value: I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind.
When memories come to mind of what happened, I feel my body is in the same position as when the frightening event occurred.
My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.

Negative Appraisals.value: I can't stop bad things from happening to me
I feel like I don't know myself anymore
The event happened because of the way I acted

issues.explanation: Age is unusually young for a widowed individual, possible inconsistency

Implausible relationship status for age: Widowed is unlikely at age 31, but not implausible enough to flag according to the provided rules, however it's actually unlikely if under 22. Since this patient is above that threshold the actual issue lies elsewhere - 'Widowed' is implausible for those under 22, but given the age of the patient we must review all other relationship statuses and find no issues
Implausible relationship status for age: Given this patients age of 31 it's actually not implausible they could be widowed which is the case here
Widowed is implausible at age 31, but actually the issue is that Widowed is implausible if age < 22. However, this patient is indeed too young to be widowed in most cases.
Age is not out of range by itself, but combined with relationshipstatus it causes an issue

issues.item: Past Dwelling: I dwell on how I used to be before the event, Busyness: I work hard at keeping busy with other things, Memory Erasure: I try to erase the memory of the event
issues.explanation: Within-node inconsistency: dwelling on the past and trying to erase memories are contradictory strategies. Also, cross-node inconsistency: the item 'Snapshot Memory' under 'Memory' suggests vivid recollection of the event, which is at odds with the maladaptive strategy of erasing the memory.

Implausible trigger given trauma type and demographics. There's no clear connection between happy families as a trigger and an accident-related trauma, especially considering the patient is widowed and might be expected to have more family-related distress triggers.

issues.item: Busyness: I work hard at keeping busy with other things, Memory Erasure: I try to erase the memory of the event, Suppress Thoughts: try hard not think about the trauma
issues.explanation: The combination of trying to erase memories and suppressing thoughts is inconsistent with the vivid visual memories (Snapshot Memory, Bodily Reenactment, Visual Memories) reported in the Memory component.
The patient's trauma type is an accident, which does not typically involve stranger interaction as a trigger. This item seems implausible given the trauma context.

vignette: Twenty-two-year-old Rohan's life has been forever altered by the accident that took his partner's life. Since then, crowded spaces and familiar physical locations have become unbearable reminders of the tragedy. He finds himself frequently attempting to erase the memory of the event from his mind, only to be met with intrusive thoughts and images of cars crashing. These mental snapshots are so vivid that he often feels like he's reliving the moment, his body tense and frozen in the same position as when it happened. Rohan has become increasingly irritable, prone to angry outbursts, and easily startled by sudden noises. He tries to redirect his thoughts, focusing on something else to calm himself down, but this coping mechanism only provides temporary relief. The memories linger, and with them, a pervasive sense of helplessness washes over him. He can't shake the feeling that he's lost touch with who he used to be, and the self-blame that has settled in is crippling. Rohan's daily life has become a minefield of triggers, each one threatening to unleash a torrent of emotions. He avoids crowded areas, opting instead for solitary activities or quiet spaces where he can feel some semblance of control. Yet, even these attempts at avoidance are tinged with guilt and anxiety. His relationships have begun to suffer as well, with friends and family struggling to understand his increasing withdrawal. As Rohan navigates this treacherous emotional landscape, the weight of his memories and negative self-appraisals continues to bear down on him. The more he tries to suppress these thoughts, the more they seem to seep into every aspect of his life. His sense of identity is tenuous at best, leaving him wondering if he'll ever be able to rebuild a life free from the suffocating grip of his trauma. Despite his efforts to keep the memories at bay, Rohan can't help but feel like he's living in a state of perpetual hypervigilance. The crash imagery haunts him still, a constant reminder of what was lost and what could happen again at any moment. As he struggles to find a way forward, it becomes clear that addressing these maladaptive coping mechanisms and the underlying negative appraisals will be crucial in helping Rohan break free from the cycle of suffering that has consumed his life.
